sources.notes: Canonical statement and constraints: choose an integer dividing point maximizing the minimum of four strict-left/inclusive-right and strict-below/inclusive-above quadrant counts; total n is at most 1e5.
Div.2 alias with the same statement; the reviewed identity remains canonical 2046C.

Official tutorial checked: it describes binary search on k with a vertical sweep and feasible y-interval intersection; its reference code implements direct monotone segment-tree descent for the best y at each x.
Opened solution cross-check: sort and group x, compress y, maintain the two x-sides with Fenwick trees, and search the crossover of the four quadrant counts in O(n log^2 n).
Re-derived the monotone lower/upper envelopes for direct descent and the exact kth-order-statistic interval used by the answer-feasibility variant.

annotation.summary: Choose an integer point (x0,y0) that partitions a multiset of planar points into four prescribed quadrants and maximizes the least quadrant size.
annotation.constraints: 4 <= n <= 1e5 per test, total n <= 1e5, coordinates are in [-1e9,1e9], duplicate points are allowed, and the output point must also stay within that coordinate range.
annotation.core_idea: Sort by x and evaluate x0 at each distinct point x before transferring that whole equal-x group, so the maintained sides exactly represent x<x0 and x>=x0. For fixed x0 define A(y)=min(L_below(y),R_below(y)) and B(y)=min(L_above(y),R_above(y)), where below means y_i<y and above means y_i>=y. A is nondecreasing, B is nonincreasing, and the score is min(A,B), so an optimum lies at their crossover. A segment tree over y stores counts for both x-sides; with accumulated counts outside the current node, descend toward larger y when A<=B and toward smaller y otherwise, checking the score at every split. As a separate feasibility variant, binary search k: for k>0, on either x-side the y0 values leaving at least k points below and above form the interval [k-th smallest y + 1, k-th largest y], and k is feasible exactly when the left and right intervals intersect for some swept x0.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory for the direct segment-tree descent; O(n log^2 n) time and O(n) memory for binary search on the answer.
tricks: evaluate before moving an entire equal-x group
encode divider ties as strict lower/left and inclusive upper/right counts
split the score into a nondecreasing below envelope and a nonincreasing above envelope
search the envelope crossover instead of ternary-searching a plateaued score
represent fixed-k feasible horizontal dividers as intersecting intervals

solution_variants.name: x-sweep-monotone-y-descent
solution_variants.summary: Sweep equal-x groups while a segment tree stores, for every y range, counts from both x-sides. At each x0, descend to the crossover between the nondecreasing minimum-below count and nonincreasing minimum-above count, updating the best score and y0 along the path.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory after sorting and coordinate compression; an implicit tree gives O(n log C) counting work plus O(n log n) sorting.

solution_variants.name: answer-binary-search-with-feasible-y-intervals
solution_variants.summary: Binary search the target k. For each check, sweep equal-x groups with two Fenwick trees, obtain the kth-smallest and kth-largest y on both x-sides, and accept an x0 when the two resulting feasible y0 intervals intersect.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log^2 n) time and O(n) memory.

tags.evidence: The algorithm enumerates all relevant vertical dividers by x while transferring point groups between maintained left and right states.
The primary variant stores y-range counts for both x-sides and descends the tree to evaluate the four quadrant counts at their optimal crossover.
For fixed x0, the minimum of the two below counts only increases with y0 while the minimum of the two above counts only decreases, locating the optimum at their crossover.
The primary variant performs a binary descent in y according to which monotone envelope currently limits the score.
The alternative variant binary-searches k because feasibility of giving every quadrant at least k points is monotone.
The alternative variant maintains both x-sides by compressed y and extracts prefix counts or kth y order statistics during every feasibility sweep.
Sorting points by x creates the sweep order, while sorted unique y values support the compressed range-count structures.
All points sharing x0 must remain on the inclusive-right side during evaluation and be transferred together afterward.
